schools.summary: Henry: Ziba, longing to be master not manager, times a seasonable gift to win David's favour and fish in troubled waters; God still overrules bad men for good.
Clarke and Ellicott detail the Eastern provisions (thin cakes, summer fruits, a wine-skin) and judge Ziba a man of no principle who backed David only from shrewdness.
Gill fixes the setting on Olivet and the servant's self-serving design.
K&D itemise the asses, bread, fruit, and wine for the faint in the wilderness; Clarke reads Ziba's self-abasing speech as the customary Eastern compliment.
Gill hears David asking whether the provisions are a gift or for sale.
K&D weigh the improbability: a cripple who never claimed the throne could not think Israel would give it him; Ziba calumniates to seize the lands.
Gill names the parties and the servant's aim at the estate.
Clarke brands Ziba a base wretch and the charge utterly unfounded against the innocent Mephibosheth.
K&D: David in his excitement was weak enough to credit Ziba and commit injustice; Ellicott renders Ziba's obsequious bow literally.
Clarke: a rash grant, spoiling an honourable man to reward a villain; afflictions make even a wise man mad; Wesley: unjust to condemn a man unheard.
Gill grants the law of forfeiture but faults David's haste in acting without inquiry.
K&D, Clarke, and Ellicott place Bahurim in Benjamin and make Shimei a kinsman of Saul, voicing the tribe's grievance that the crown had passed away.
Henry's governing contrast: David bore Shimei's curses better than Ziba's flatteries; the world's smiles are more dangerous than its frowns.
Gill sets David in the fields near, not yet in, the town.
Ellicott explains the ravine that kept Shimei out of reach; K&D find it impossible to determine exactly what 'blood of Saul's house' Shimei means.
Gill: the stones were contempt, not assault, hinting at David's sin with Bathsheba.
Henry: those who fight against God cannot hurt him though they hate him; the malice is odious yet impotent and ridiculous.
Gill and Ellicott render 'Go out, go out'; Ellicott weighs whether 'bloody man' means only Saul's house or also Uriah's blood.
Henry: cursing the ruler broke the law; David was in fact innocent of Saul's blood, having twice spared his life — innocency is no fence against slander.

debates.question: What blood does Shimei mean by calling David a 'bloody man'?

sides.position: only 'the blood of the house of Saul' — Ishbosheth, Abner, and the seven Gibeonite executions
possibly also the innocent blood of Uriah

schools.summary: Gill exposes the false insinuations — the Philistine war, the deaths of Ishbosheth and Abner, the Gibeonite executions.
Wesley: 'taken in thy mischief' gloats that David's own violence has returned on his head.
Henry: Shimei presses providence into private revenge; the wrath of man works not the righteousness of God, nor does God's righteousness serve man's wrath.
K&D catch the contempt in 'dead dog' (as at 2 Sam 9:8) and hear Joab behind Abishai's request to behead Shimei.
Gill: Abishai, David's nephew and a general, could not bear to hear the king abused.
Henry: the sons of Zeruiah would defend David's honour with the sword, as the disciples would call down fire; David will not suffer it.
K&D read 'look upon my guilt' (not 'mine eye'); Ellicott: David recognises all his sufferings as from the Lord's hand and submits without excusing Shimei.
Clarke argues the particle is 'for if' not 'because'; Wesley: God spoke by providence, not precept — he did not put wickedness in Shimei's heart.
Gill: David refuses his generals' revenge, for the cursing cannot hurt him.

debates.question: How is 'the Lord hath said unto him, Curse David' to be read?

sides.position: render the particle 'for if', so as never to make God the author of the curse
keep 'because', understanding that God spoke by providence permitting, not by precept commanding

schools.summary: Wesley: a son's plot is worse than words, and the Benjamite is of the dispossessed house; Clarke reads David as chastened of God, guilty of Uriah's blood though clear of Saul's.
Ellicott contrasts the Benjamite with David's own son, noting David charges Absalom with seeking his life, not only his throne.
Henry: tribulation works patience; the more we bear the abler we grow to bear more.
Gill: David speaks to reconcile Abishai and the servants to the abuse.

outliers.note: Inserts Charles Wesley's hymn paraphrase of David's mind ('Let Shimei curse: the rod he bears / For sins which mercy had forgiven').

schools.summary: Ellicott prefers the written text 'mine iniquity'; K&D picture Shimei pacing the opposite hill, David walking on and casting his cause on God.
Gill gives 'mine affliction' and the variant 'mine eye' — the tear wrung out by his troubles.

debates.question: What is the true reading — 'mine affliction', 'mine eye', or 'mine iniquity'?

sides.position: 'mine affliction', following the Septuagint and Vulgate
'mine iniquity' — the written Hebrew text, probably the true sense
'mine eye', i.e. my tears (the Targum and the rabbis)

themes: waiting for God to requite good

schools.summary: Gill: David took no notice, which only made Shimei bolder; he marks the scribal pause set in mid-verse.
K&D fix the picture — Shimei on the opposite slope across the ravine, cursing and flinging stones and earth 'over against' the king.
K&D and Ellicott argue the word for 'weary' is the place-name Ayephim, since 'there' needs an antecedent and the halt lay beyond Bahurim toward Jordan.
Gill takes it as the plain adjective: they came weary with journey, grief, and trouble.

debates.question: Is the word for 'weary' a common adjective or the place-name Ayephim?

sides.position: a proper name, Ayephim — a resting-place or caravansary
the plain adjective 'weary', describing the travellers

schools.summary: Henry: God lets the wicked prosper awhile that their disappointment be the more grievous; Absalom's two celebrated counsellors will prove miserable ones.
K&D note the rebel host and Hushai's greeting; Clarke observes 'the men of Israel' is absent from several versions and manuscripts.
Gill: Absalom reached Jerusalem the very hour David reached Bahurim — a hair's breadth from capture.

themes: the triumph of the wicked is short

schools.summary: Ellicott restores 'Let the king live' and reads Hushai's plea of loyalty to the government de facto; K&D expose the 'great craftiness' of naming Absalom as Jehovah's chosen.
Gill: David's friend took the first opportunity to make his court to the usurper.
Gill: Absalom will not name David as father or king, only as 'Hushai's friend'; the charge of ingratitude rebounds on the rebel son.
Wesley: the question is only a test; 'thy friend' is a sneer against a David kind to strangers but not to his own son.
Henry: Absalom easily believes what he wishes true — the pride of his heart deceived him.
K&D render the pledge in full, pitched so Absalom hears himself as the Lord's chosen while the words hold true of David.
Clarke lays bare the equivocation — Hushai means David but speaks so Absalom thinks himself meant; the insincerity is Hushai's own to answer for.
Gill marks it as the opening of Hushai's answer.
Gill lays out the logic: a second reason for service — loyalty passing naturally from the father to the son.
K&D hear the same double meaning — service vowed to the son yet secretly kept toward the father.
Clarke warns that this too is to be understood in the equivocal way of the previous verse.
K&D: with the capital taken, the plan now is to take and keep the throne; the plural 'give ye' includes the others present.
Gill: Absalom turns to Ahithophel as his first and chief counsellor.
Henry: Ahithophel was cried up as an oracle, yet worldly wisdom without grace acts most foolishly — the greatest statesmen are seldom the greatest saints.
K&D and Ellicott: seizing the harem was a complete usurpation, meant to make the breach irreconcilable and steel the rebels; an Oriental custom blunted by polygamy.
Clarke: the sum of abomination, death by the law, done where David eyed Bathsheba and fulfilling Nathan's word; Wesley adds Ahithophel's revenge for his granddaughter.
Gill: Ahithophel gave the counsel, whether alone or as the council's mouth.
Gill: the roof was the very spot where David first saw Bathsheba.
K&D: the tent was pitched for public view; Ellicott: the public punishment answers the secret crime on the very roof where David sinned.
Wesley: a public claim to the kingdom, and the exact fulfilment of Nathan's threat — the Lord is righteous.
Henry: Absalom threw away the scabbard and declared his sin as Sodom, yet the word of God was fulfilled to the letter.

themes: sin answered on the very spot

schools.summary: K&D render the emphatic apposition and explain the note accounts for why so foul a counsel won ready approval.
Gill: his oracular repute held both with David and with Absalom, before and after the revolt.
Wesley: his counsel was received with veneration and reckoned as sure of success — authority, not merit, carried it.
